title: India's Basmati Rice Export in 2026: New Floor Prices, Top Markets, and Buyer Strategies
excerpt: India's government has revised the Minimum Export Price (MEP) for Basmati rice in 2026. We analyze how the new pricing floor affects importers in the Middle East, EU, and USA. With 1121 and 1718 varieties dominating, we break down current FOB rates per MT, container capacities, and the best procurement windows for buyers looking to lock in annual contracts. Key insight: 1718 Basmati is now outselling 1121 in several GCC markets due to superior aroma at competitive pricing.
author: Radiance Export Team

sections.heading: India's 2026 Basmati MEP: What Changed
sections.body: India's Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) revised the Minimum Export Price (MEP) for Basmati rice in early 2026, setting a new floor that is reshaping procurement strategies from Riyadh to Rotterdam. The revision — the third MEP adjustment in 36 months — signals that the Indian government is balancing domestic food security with its ambition to grow Basmati's global premium positioning. For importers running annual volume contracts, the change requires a hard look at current FOB benchmarks versus the new floor.

sections.caption: 1718 Basmati paddy field in Punjab — the variety now outselling 1121 in several Gulf markets due to superior aroma.

sections.heading: 1718 vs 1121: Why GCC Buyers Are Switching
sections.body: The 2026 market has seen a notable shift in GCC buyer preference from 1121 Basmati toward 1718 Basmati. The reason is straightforward: 1718 offers a more pronounced floral aroma profile — the traditional hallmark of premium Basmati — at a price point that has converged closer to 1121 as acreage of 1718 expanded in Haryana and Punjab. Head rice recovery is also better for 1718 (typically 2–3% higher), making it more cost-efficient for millers and ultimately for importers calculating cost-per-usable-MT.

sections.label: Procurement Timing Tip
sections.text: October–December is historically the best FOB window for Basmati. Fresh kharif crop arrivals at Punjab mills in October create temporary price softening. Buyers who lock annual contracts in this window typically save $40–80/MT vs. off-season procurement.

sections.heading: Documentation for EU and US Basmati Entry
sections.body: EU Basmati imports require: APEDA Certificate of Origin (mandatory for preferential duty under India-EU trade terms), pesticide residue test per EC Regulation 396/2005 from a NABL-accredited lab, and Phytosanitary Certificate. For the USA: FDA Prior Notice submission (at least 4–6 hours before container arrival), Bill of Lading, commercial invoice, and a NABL COA covering moisture, broken %, and pesticide panel. Radiance Overseas provides full documentation sets with every shipment — buyers receive a digital documentation package 5 days before vessel departure.

title: Global Cumin Shortage 2026: India's Rajasthan Crop Falls 30% — Impact on Importers
excerpt: Unseasonal rainfall in Rajasthan and Gujarat has damaged the 2026 cumin crop, with production estimates down 30% from last year. This is pushing cumin prices to multi-year highs across all grades — Singapore, Europe, and USA quality. We analyze the supply deficit, price forecasts through Q3 2026, alternative sourcing from Syria and Turkey, and recommend hedging strategies for bulk cumin importers. Volatile oil content and physical purity specs remain stable for available stock.
author: Radiance Spice Desk

sections.heading: The 2026 Cumin Deficit: Causes and Consequences
sections.body: The 2026 cumin season in India has produced a supply shock not seen since 2012. Erratic March rainfall in Rajasthan's Barmer and Jodhpur districts — the heart of Indian cumin cultivation — damaged standing crops during the critical flowering stage. NCDEX warehouse receipts show arrivals down more than 30% year-on-year. Spot prices at Unjha mandi (Gujarat) have breached ₹55,000/quintal, a level not seen in a decade. For international buyers who run 6–12 month forward contracts, the question is not whether prices will rise, but by how much and for how long.

sections.caption: Cumin seeds drying on jute sheets in Rajasthan — the 2026 damaged crop has pushed prices to decade highs.

sections.heading: Price Forecast Through Q3 2026
sections.body: Based on current mandi arrivals and forward contracts, cumin prices are expected to remain elevated through September 2026. The Syria and Turkey alternate origins — which historically provide a partial price ceiling — are themselves in short supply in 2026 due to geopolitical and drought factors. This creates a rare scenario where all three major cumin origins face concurrent tightness. Buyers with Q3 or Q4 requirements are advised to cover at least 50% of volume at current spot levels rather than waiting for a correction that may not materialise before the new crop in early 2027.

sections.label: Risk Management for Cumin Buyers
sections.text: Split your procurement: book 50% at current CIF and leave 50% open. Set price alerts at Unjha for ₹48,000/quintal — a dip to that level would represent a 12% correction and is a reasonable re-entry point. Always specify volatile oil content ≥2.5% in your purchase contract to ensure aromatic quality.

sections.heading: Specifications That Protect Buyers
sections.body: In tight supply markets, unscrupulous sellers may offer off-spec cumin to move inventory. Protect yourself by specifying in writing: moisture max 9%, volatile oil min 2.5%, admixture max 2%, black seeds max 1%, Singapore/Europe quality grade, and NABL pesticide residue certificate (chlorpyrifos, profenofos as priority analytes). Radiance Overseas guarantees spec compliance with a 100% replacement policy on any non-conforming lot.

title: Indian Specialty Coffee Exports Surge 40%: How Coorg and Chikmagalur Are Going Global
excerpt: India's specialty coffee exports grew 40% year-on-year in FY2025, driven by demand from European and Japanese roasters for single-origin, shade-grown Arabica from Coorg (Kodagu) and Chikmagalur. The Coffee Board of India reports that Arabica Plantation A and Peaberry grades are achieving premium pricing 20-30% above standard FOB rates. We profile the key growing regions, processing methods (washed vs natural), cup profiles, and how importers can source directly from estate-level producers through aggregators like Radiance Overseas.
author: Coffee Sourcing Team

sections.heading: India's Specialty Coffee Moment
sections.body: For decades, India was known internationally primarily as a commodity Robusta supplier for espresso blends. That perception is changing rapidly. FY2025 data from the Coffee Board of India shows specialty and premium green coffee exports grew 40% year-on-year, with single-origin Arabica from Coorg (Kodagu) district and Chikmagalur commanding Cup of Excellence-adjacent scores. European micro-roasters, Japanese café chains, and North American specialty importers are now actively building India sourcing programs — many for the first time.

sections.caption: Hand-harvested Arabica cherries from an estate in Chikmagalur — shade-grown under native forest canopy at 1,000–1,500m elevation.

sections.heading: Coorg vs Chikmagalur: Cup Profile Differences
sections.body: Both districts produce shade-grown Arabica at elevations between 900m and 1,500m, but the cup profiles diverge meaningfully. Coorg Arabica tends to produce a heavier body with caramel sweetness, lower acidity, and chocolate-forward notes — well-suited to filter and pour-over applications. Chikmagalur Arabica, especially from the higher estates above 1,200m, shows brighter citric acidity, floral aromatics, and a cleaner finish that appeals to light-roast specialty buyers. For Peaberry lots from either origin, expect a more concentrated flavour intensity — these command 20–25% price premiums.

sections.label: Specialty Sourcing Requirements
sections.text: Request the producer's green bean moisture content report (target 10–12%), screen size distribution, defect count (SCAA methodology), and a roasted cup report if available. For EU organic buyers, ensure the estate holds a current NPOP certificate with transaction certificate issued for the specific lot.

sections.heading: Processing Methods and Their Market Fit
sections.body: Indian specialty coffee is predominantly washed (wet) processed, producing the clean, bright cup that specialty roasters seek. Natural (dry) processed Indian Arabica is rarer but growing — the Karnataka Climate Resilience project has encouraged more estates to experiment, producing fruit-forward, wine-like cup profiles that fetch significant premiums in the Nordic and Japanese markets. Honey-processed lots are also emerging. For institutional buyers (hospitality, café chains), washed lots offer the most consistency. For specialty retail, natural and honey-process command the highest retail price points.

title: EU's New Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) and Its Impact on Indian Agro Exports
excerpt: The European Union Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), effective from December 2025, requires importers to prove that agricultural products are deforestation-free. While India's agro exports — rice, spices, coffee, pulses — are largely from established agricultural land, the documentation requirements have changed. We explain what the EUDR means for Indian exporters, which product categories are affected, what due diligence documentation importers need, and how Radiance Overseas has adapted its traceability systems to ensure EUDR compliance for all EU-bound shipments.
author: Compliance Team

sections.heading: EUDR and Indian Agro Exports: The New Reality
sections.body: The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), which took effect in December 2025, requires EU importers to prove that seven commodity categories — including coffee, cocoa, and soy — did not contribute to deforestation. For Indian exporters, the regulation creates a documentation challenge rather than a fundamental sourcing problem, since India's agricultural land has been stable for decades. However, the proof requirements are detailed and new — and non-compliance means your shipment can be seized at EU ports with no right to compensation.

sections.caption: Satellite-mapped organic farm in Madhya Pradesh — geo-coordinates are now required for EUDR-compliant coffee and soy shipments to the EU.

sections.heading: Which Indian Products Are Affected
sections.body: Of the seven EUDR commodity categories, two are significant Indian exports: coffee (Karnataka, Kerala) and soy. Rubber, cattle, cocoa, palm oil, and wood are either not applicable or negligible for India. For Indian coffee exporters, EUDR requires: GPS coordinates for every farm in the supply chain, a due diligence statement confirming deforestation-free status, and supply chain documentation traceable to farm level. For soy (used in animal feed), similar farm-level traceability is required. Spices, rice, pulses, and sesame are not currently in scope but may be added in future reviews.

sections.label: EUDR Compliance Checklist for Coffee Buyers
sections.text: Before placing any EU-bound Indian coffee order after December 2025: (1) Confirm supplier has GPS coordinates for all source farms. (2) Request the due diligence statement (DDS) they will file with EU customs. (3) Verify the statement covers your specific contract lot, not just a generic certificate. Radiance Overseas has pre-filed EUDR documentation for all EU-bound coffee and has farm GPS data for 100% of source estates.

sections.heading: Documentation Protocol at Radiance Overseas
sections.body: Radiance Overseas implemented EUDR compliance protocols from January 2025, well ahead of the December 2025 deadline. Our compliance stack includes: farm-level GPS mapping (via mobile geo-tagging of all contracted farms), supply chain documentation from farm to factory, due diligence statements filed per shipment via the EU TRACES system, and NABL-accredited testing for pesticide residues. Buyers receive the complete EUDR documentation package alongside standard shipping documents — no additional requests needed.

title: Indian Sesame Seeds Hit Record Export Volume: Tahini Demand Drives Growth
excerpt: India exported over 350,000 MT of sesame seeds in 2025, a new record driven by surging tahini and hummus consumption in the Middle East, Europe, and North America. Hulled sesame 99.95% purity remains the dominant export grade, with Gujarat and Rajasthan accounting for 85% of production. We analyze the price dynamics between Indian, Ethiopian, and Sudanese sesame, the impact of El Nino on the 2026 crop outlook, and quality benchmarks that buyers should specify for food-grade applications including FFA, moisture, and microbiological limits.
author: Radiance Oil Seeds Desk

sections.heading: India's Sesame Export Record: The Tahini Economy
sections.body: India exported over 350,000 metric tonnes of sesame seeds in 2025 — a new all-time record driven by one dominant trend: the global tahini and hummus consumption boom. Middle Eastern diaspora populations in Europe and North America have mainstreamed tahini from a niche ingredient to a supermarket staple. Israeli, Turkish, and Lebanese food brands are scaling production, creating insatiable demand for hulled sesame 99.95% purity — the grade India produces best. Gujarat and Rajasthan together account for 85% of India's sesame output, and the Unjha market is the global price-setting hub.

sections.caption: Premium hulled sesame seeds from Unjha, Gujarat — 99.95% purity, tested for FFA, moisture, and heavy metals before export.

sections.heading: Sesame Grades: Which to Specify for Your Application
sections.body: Hulled Sesame 99.95% is mandatory for tahini and direct food applications — specify moisture ≤5%, FFA ≤1.0%, oil content ≥48%, and peroxide value ≤5. Natural White Sesame (with hull) suits oil crushing and traditional baking — lower price, higher moisture allowance (≤6%). Black Sesame is a premium health-food and bakery ingredient — origin Gujarat, specify uniform jet-black colour, no admixture of white seeds. For sesame oil extraction, natural white is more economical. For tahini, only hulled 99.95% meets the FFA and colour profile required.

sections.label: New Crop vs Carry-over Stock
sections.text: Always specify 'new crop' and the crop year in your Purchase Order. Carry-over sesame from the previous season shows elevated FFA and reduced oil quality. India's sesame harvest is in November–January (Gujarat/Rajasthan). Fresh-crop availability begins December — this is the optimal window for Q1 orders.

sections.heading: El Niño and the 2026 Crop Outlook
sections.body: Meteorological modelling for kharif 2026 suggests a weakened southwest monsoon pattern over Saurashtra — the primary sesame growing region. Early estimates project 8–12% lower sesame sown area versus 2025, as farmers shift toward more drought-tolerant crops. For importers with Q2–Q3 2026 requirements, forward booking at current new-crop prices is advisable. Prices have already started reflecting this outlook, with Unjha spot up approximately 8% from the December 2025 post-harvest lows.

title: Red Sea Crisis and Indian Agro Exports: Freight Rates, Delays, and Route Alternatives
excerpt: The ongoing Red Sea shipping disruption continues to impact Indian agro exports to Europe and the Mediterranean. Container vessels are rerouting via the Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 days transit time and $1,500-2,500 per TEU in additional freight costs. We analyze the impact on CIF pricing for rice, spices, and coffee shipments from JNPT and Mundra ports, the shift in buyer preference toward larger single shipments to amortize freight costs, and when the situation is expected to normalize. Alternative routing through Haifa and overland corridors is also discussed.
author: Logistics Team

sections.heading: Red Sea Disruption: Impact on Indore to Europe Corridors
sections.body: The ongoing security situation in the Red Sea has forced almost all major container shipping lines to reroute via the Cape of Good Hope. For Indian agro exporters — particularly those shipping from JNPT and Mundra to European and Mediterranean ports — this adds 10 to 14 days of transit time and between USD 1,500 and 2,500 per TEU in additional bunker and time costs. The practical impact: CIF prices to Rotterdam, Hamburg, and Genoa have risen materially, and buyers who had locked CIF contracts are renegotiating or absorbing significant losses on thin margins.

sections.caption: Container vessel rerouting via Cape of Good Hope — Indian agro exporters face longer transit times and higher freight costs to Europe.

sections.heading: How to Reprice: FOB vs CIF in This Environment
sections.body: For buyers who currently source on CIF terms, the Red Sea disruption has effectively made their contracts more expensive — the freight component embedded in CIF pricing has blown out. A practical mitigation: shift new contracts to FOB Mundra or FOB JNPT and engage your own freight forwarder to lock rates independently. FOB pricing is currently more stable and allows buyers to benefit from any freight rate normalisation without renegotiating the base commodity price. Radiance Overseas is comfortable with both FOB and CIF terms and can advise on current freight rate benchmarks.

sections.label: Shelf Life Planning for Red Sea Rerouting
sections.text: With 10–14 extra days in transit, ensure your products have adequate remaining shelf life on arrival. Dehydrated products: check moisture spec is tight (≤5%). Sesame: ensure FFA was low at loading (≤0.5% for 14-day buffer). Rice: parboiled Sella grades handle extended transit better than Raw/Steam. Request a moisture cert at loading, not just the NABL pre-shipment cert.

sections.heading: Alternative Routing and Multi-Modal Options
sections.body: A small but growing portion of India-Europe agro trade is moving via Iran-Turkey overland transit and the India-Middle East-Europe (IMEC) corridor — but these are not yet scalable for bulk commodity containers. For time-sensitive shipments (certified organic lots with tight transaction certificate validity windows), air freight for samples and documents is being used while main cargo moves by sea. Most buyers are simply accepting the longer transit and adjusting payment terms to account for the delay.

title: Ashwagandha Extract Market Hits $1.5 Billion: India's Position as Global Supplier
excerpt: The global ashwagandha market is projected to reach $1.5 billion by 2027, and India controls over 80% of raw material supply. Madhya Pradesh and Rajasthan dominate production, with Nagori and regular varieties offering different withanolide concentrations. We examine the three key market segments — root powder (1.5% withanolides), concentrated extract (5%), and KSM-66 full-spectrum extract (>5%) — pricing differences, USDA organic certification requirements for the US supplement market, and why European buyers increasingly demand heavy metal and pesticide residue testing beyond standard NABL COA parameters.
author: Herbs & Pharma Desk

sections.heading: Ashwagandha: India's $1.5 Billion Botanical Export
sections.body: The global ashwagandha market is on track to reach USD 1.5 billion by 2027, according to multiple independent market research firms. India controls over 80% of raw material supply, with Madhya Pradesh and Rajasthan the dominant producing states. What makes the current market unique is the simultaneous boom across three distinct customer segments: pharmaceutical-grade extract buyers in Europe, USDA NOP-certified organic supplement brands in North America, and mainstream food and beverage companies incorporating ashwagandha into functional foods, sleep aids, and stress-relief products.

sections.caption: Ashwagandha root harvest in Madhya Pradesh — India's dominant ashwagandha belt produces both Nagori and regular varieties.

sections.heading: Root Powder vs Extract: What to Specify
sections.body: Three distinct product forms dominate ashwagandha trade: Root Powder (1.5% withanolides) — the most affordable form; used in traditional Ayurvedic formulations, protein powders, and value-tier supplements. Standardised Root Extract (5% withanolides) — HPLC-verified concentration; used by supplement brands seeking mid-tier clinical credibility. KSM-66 / KSM-equivalent Full-Spectrum Extract (≥5% withanolides) — the benchmark for clinical studies; proprietary process from root only (no leaf or stem); commands significant price premiums and is the only form some European regulators accept for health claims. Always specify withanolide % and the assay method (HPLC, not gravimetric).

sections.label: Heavy Metal Compliance is Non-Negotiable
sections.text: EU and US supplement buyers must require an ICP-MS heavy metals panel (arsenic, cadmium, lead, mercury) on every ashwagandha lot. Indian soils in certain growing areas have naturally elevated heavy metals. NABL-accredited ICP-MS testing (not rapid test kits) is the only acceptable method for US and EU compliance. Always verify the testing lab's NABL accreditation number against the NABL portal before accepting a COA.

sections.heading: Organic Certification and US Market Entry
sections.body: Ashwagandha sold as 'organic' in the USA must carry USDA NOP certification — the India-US equivalency arrangement does not exist. A US-accredited NOP certifier (e.g., CCOF, QCS, or OEFFA) must have audited the farm and processing facility. Radiance Overseas holds USDA NOP certification for ashwagandha root powder from our Madhya Pradesh supply chain, with transaction certificates issued per lot. For EU entry, our NPOP certification is fully equivalent under the India-EU bilateral agreement.

title: India's Chickpea Export Policy 2025-26: Kabuli vs Desi — What's Freely Exportable?
excerpt: India has updated its pulse export policy for FY2025-26, removing quantity restrictions on kabuli chickpeas while maintaining a floor price on desi chana. This policy shift is significant for Middle Eastern hummus manufacturers and EU food processors who rely on Indian 42/44 count kabuli chickpeas. We detail the current export regulations, available stock estimates from MP and Rajasthan, price comparison with Australian and Canadian chickpeas, and why Indian kabuli is gaining market share due to competitive pricing and improving quality grading infrastructure.
author: Pulses Export Team

sections.heading: India's Chickpea Export Policy Shift: What It Means for Buyers
sections.body: India's updated pulse export policy for FY2025-26 has significantly improved the outlook for kabuli chickpea importers. After two years of floor prices and quota restrictions, the government has removed quantity limits on kabuli chickpeas while retaining a floor price on desi chana (to protect domestic supply). This policy distinction matters enormously: kabuli chickpeas (42/44, 44/46 count) — the variety used in hummus, canning, and EU food processing — are now freely exportable at market-determined prices. Desi chana remains more restricted and is subject to policy changes with little notice.

sections.caption: Kabuli chickpeas 42/44 count from Madhya Pradesh — freely exportable in FY2025-26 and competitively priced vs Australian origin.

sections.heading: Indian vs Australian Kabuli: A Price Comparison
sections.body: Australia is India's main competitor in global kabuli chickpea exports. In most years, Australian chickpeas command a slight quality premium due to more uniform sizing and lower broken percentage. However, in 2025-26, Indian kabuli from MP is closing this gap. Indian 42/44 kabuli is currently available at approximately 15–18% below Australian CIF prices to Middle Eastern ports and 12–15% below Australian CIF to EU ports. Protein content (21–23% for Indian Nagori/Kabuli) is comparable to Australian origin. For price-sensitive hummus manufacturers and canning operations, Indian kabuli now offers a compelling value proposition.

sections.label: Specification Tip for Hummus and Canning Buyers
sections.text: For hummus manufacturing, specify: moisture ≤12%, protein ≥21% (dry basis), broken ≤0.5%, foreign matter ≤0.1%, count 42/44 or 44/46 per ounce (28g). For canning applications, add hydration ratio specification (minimum 1:1.8 weight after soaking 12 hours). Radiance Overseas pre-tests every kabuli lot for hydration ratio on request.

sections.heading: Procurement Calendar and Available Stock
sections.body: Kabuli chickpea harvest in Rajasthan and MP concludes in March–April. Best procurement window is April–July when new crop is available in bulk and prices reflect post-harvest softness. From August onwards, carry-over stock prices typically rise 5–8% as domestic demand absorbs supply. For buyers running long-term contracts (12-month rolling), we recommend locking the base price during April–June and scheduling quarterly shipments through March of the following year.

title: NPOP to PGS-India: Understanding India's Organic Certification Landscape in 2025
excerpt: India operates two parallel organic certification systems — NPOP (National Programme for Organic Production) for third-party certified organic, and PGS-India (Participatory Guarantee System) for smallholder farmer groups. For international trade, only NPOP certification is recognized for export. We explain the critical differences, why importers must verify that their Indian supplier holds NPOP (not just PGS) certification, how the Transaction Certificate (TC) system works for each shipment, and the list of NPOP-accredited certifying bodies that EU and USDA recognize. Common compliance pitfalls that delay customs clearance are also covered.
author: Compliance Team

sections.heading: NPOP vs PGS-India: A Critical Distinction for Import Compliance
sections.body: India operates two parallel organic certification frameworks, and confusing them can cause serious customs problems. The NPOP (National Programme for Organic Production) is the third-party, internationally recognised certification system administered by APEDA. The PGS-India (Participatory Guarantee System) is a peer-review system designed for smallholder farmers selling domestically. They are not equivalent. Only NPOP-certified products with a valid Transaction Certificate can be imported as 'organic' into the EU, USA (with caveats), UK, and other regulated markets.

sections.caption: NPOP organic certification documentation — every organic export shipment requires both the annual certificate and a per-shipment Transaction Certificate.

sections.heading: The Transaction Certificate: Why It's Non-Negotiable
sections.body: The most common compliance failure in organic agro imports is the absence of a valid Transaction Certificate (TC). The annual NPOP certificate proves the farm or processor is certified — but the TC proves that this specific shipment comes from certified inputs and has maintained organic integrity through processing and export. EU customs authorities routinely reject organic claims when only the annual certificate is presented without the corresponding TC. The TC must be issued by the NPOP-accredited certifying body (e.g., Control Union, ECOCERT, OneCert) for each transaction and must match the shipment quantity and product description exactly.

sections.label: What to Verify Before Accepting an Organic Lot
sections.text: Before confirming any organic purchase order: (1) Verify the supplier's annual NPOP certificate is current and covers the specific product. (2) Confirm the certifying body is NPOP-accredited — check the APEDA NPOP website for the current list. (3) Request the TC with your company as the buyer before the vessel sails — not after. (4) Cross-check the TC lot number against the Packing List and Commercial Invoice. Radiance Overseas provides TCs as standard documentation for every organic shipment.

sections.heading: EU vs US vs Canada Organic Entry Requirements
sections.body: For EU entry: NPOP is equivalent to EU Organic (Commission Regulation). Present the TC and the Certificate of Inspection issued under EU import rules. For US entry: India does not have an NOP equivalency arrangement. Your Indian supplier must hold a separate USDA NOP certificate from a US-accredited certifier. Using only NPOP for a product sold as 'organic' in the USA is a violation of 7 CFR 205. For Canada: The Canada Organic Regime (COPROC) has a bilateral equivalency with India's NPOP — similar documentation to EU applies.

sections.heading: Certified Organic Products from Radiance Overseas

title: Dehydrated Onion and Garlic: India's $800 Million Export Opportunity
excerpt: India's dehydrated onion and garlic exports exceeded $800 million in FY2025, with Maharashtra's Nashik-Malegaon belt producing over 60% of global supply. The instant noodle, ready-meal, and seasoning industries are the primary demand drivers. We analyze the quality grades (minced, flakes, granules, powder), moisture and SO2 specifications that different markets require, the price advantage of Indian dehy products vs Chinese competitors, and why major food manufacturers like Nestle, Unilever, and ITC are increasingly sourcing from certified Indian producers. Infrastructure investments in Nashik's dehy processing cluster are creating new capacity for 2026.
author: Dehydrated Products Team

sections.heading: India's $800 Million Dehydrated Vegetable Export Sector
sections.body: Dehydrated onion and garlic exports from India crossed USD 800 million in FY2025, establishing India firmly as the world's dominant supplier. The Nashik-Malegaon belt in Maharashtra processes over 60% of global dehydrated onion supply, while Rajasthan's Barmer and Jodhpur districts supply the majority of dehydrated garlic. The demand base spans some of the world's largest food companies — Nestlé, Unilever, ITC, McCormick — who rely on Indian dehydrated vegetables as a reliable, scalable, and cost-effective ingredient for instant noodles, ready meals, pizza seasonings, and soups.

sections.caption: Dehydrated onion flakes being dried at a modern processing facility in Nashik — India's capital of dehydrated vegetable production.

sections.heading: Product Forms and Grade Specifications
sections.body: Dehydrated Onion: Available as minced (3–5mm), flakes (10–15mm), granules (1–3mm), and powder (40–80 mesh). Moisture max 5%, SO₂ content max 50 ppm for EU standard (75 ppm for non-EU markets), colour white/golden, TPC max 10⁵ CFU/g, Salmonella absent. Dehydrated Garlic: Flakes, granules (8/14 or 24/40 mesh), and powder (60–80 mesh). Moisture max 6%, allicin content specification available, AMS (allyl methyl sulfide) profile on request for nutraceutical buyers. Always specify the mesh size, not just the form — 'granule' without mesh size creates ambiguity that leads to wrong product delivery.

sections.label: SO₂ Specification for EU vs Non-EU Markets
sections.text: EU Directive 95/2/EC limits SO₂ in dehydrated onion to 50 ppm. US FDA has a 75 ppm limit. Some Middle Eastern markets accept up to 100 ppm. Always state your market's permitted level in the Purchase Order. Specifying 'EU grade' (50 ppm) for an EU-bound order is the single most important specification to include to avoid customs rejection.

sections.heading: New Processing Capacity and 2026 Supply Outlook
sections.body: The Nashik processing cluster has seen significant infrastructure investment since 2023, with three new large-scale processing facilities coming online in 2025–26. This adds approximately 25,000 MT/year of additional hulling and drying capacity for onion, reducing the seasonal price spikes that historically occurred when fresh onion arrivals exceeded drying capacity. For 2026, supply is expected to be stable with moderate pricing — the onion kharif crop in Maharashtra has been satisfactory. Dehydrated garlic from Rajasthan faces tighter supply due to lower rabi plantings, and prices are 10–15% above 2025 levels.

title: Non-Basmati Rice Export from India: IR 64, Swarna, and Parboiled Grades for African Markets
excerpt: Africa imports over 15 million MT of rice annually, and India is the dominant supplier of parboiled non-basmati grades. IR 64 Parboiled, PR 11, and Swarna are the workhorse varieties, shipping from JNPT and Mundra at 25 MT per 20-foot container. We break down the quality specifications (broken percentage, moisture, color) that Nigerian, Ghanaian, and Beninese importers require, current FOB and CIF pricing, the role of pre-shipment inspection (SGS/Bureau Veritas), and how India's non-basmati export policy has evolved since the 2023 ban. Demand from East African markets — Tanzania, Kenya, Madagascar — is the newest growth segment.
author: Rice Export Desk

sections.heading: India's Non-Basmati Rice: The Backbone of African Food Security
sections.body: Africa imports over 15 million metric tonnes of rice annually, and India supplies the dominant share through parboiled and raw non-basmati grades. IR 64 Parboiled, PR 11, Swarna, and Long Grain Parboiled are the workhorses of this trade — high-volume, price-sensitive, and shipped from both JNPT and Mundra in 20-foot and 40-foot containers. Nigeria alone imports approximately 3 million MT/year, primarily from India, making it India's largest single non-basmati buyer. The trade is fundamentally different from Basmati — it is volume-driven, price-sensitive, and requires reliable pre-shipment inspection to prevent quality disputes.

sections.caption: Parboiled IR 64 rice — the standard grade for West African markets, distinguished by its golden amber colour and firm cooked texture.

sections.heading: Grade Specifications for Key African Markets
sections.body: Nigeria and West Africa: IR 64 Parboiled with 5% broken maximum (allowance up to 15% in budget trades), moisture max 14%, admixture max 2%, golden amber colour, 25 MT per 20-foot container. East Africa (Kenya, Tanzania): Both parboiled and raw white rice are accepted; Long Grain White Raw with 5% broken is growing. Benin (re-export hub): Swarna and PR 11 parboiled at 15–25% broken are common for transit trade. Always request a pre-shipment inspection (SGS or Bureau Veritas) for African buyers — it protects both parties and is standard practice for all reputable Indian exporters.

sections.label: Pre-shipment Inspection Protocol
sections.text: For non-basmati rice export to Africa, SGS or Bureau Veritas pre-shipment inspection is strongly recommended. Key parameters to inspect at the stuffing point: moisture %, broken %, whiteness/colour, admixture, and fumigation. Request the inspection certificate before the Bill of Lading is issued — this is your documentary protection against destination claims.

sections.heading: India's Export Policy for Non-Basmati: 2024–2026 Update
sections.body: India banned non-basmati white rice exports in August 2023 and partially lifted the ban in October 2024 with minimum export price conditions. By early 2025, the export ban was fully removed for all grades. Parboiled rice export has remained unrestricted throughout this period. For African buyers who experienced supply disruptions in 2023–24, the current open export window represents the best opportunity in two years to re-establish supply contracts with Indian exporters. Radiance Overseas maintained supply continuity to African buyers during the restriction period through parboiled grades.

title: Aflatoxin and Pesticide Residue Testing: MRL Compliance for Indian Agro Exports
excerpt: Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) for pesticides and aflatoxin levels are the number one reason for shipment rejection at EU and US customs. The EU's Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed (RASFF) flagged 47 Indian agro shipments in 2024 for MRL non-compliance — primarily spices and groundnuts. We explain the EU MRL database, US FDA detention thresholds, NABL lab testing protocols that exporters must follow, the critical difference between NABL and non-NABL certificates, and how Radiance Overseas implements a three-stage testing protocol (farm-gate, pre-processing, and pre-shipment) to ensure zero rejections. Specific MRL limits for turmeric, cumin, black pepper, and groundnuts are tabulated.
author: Quality Assurance Team

sections.heading: MRL Compliance: India's Biggest Export Quality Challenge
sections.body: Maximum Residue Limits (MRL) for pesticides and aflatoxin contamination are the primary cause of Indian agro shipment rejections at EU and US customs. The EU's RASFF (Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed) flagged 47 Indian agro shipments in 2024 for MRL non-compliance — predominantly in spices (cumin, chilli, turmeric) and groundnuts. These rejections cost exporters USD 2,000–15,000 per container in border control costs, re-export or destruction, and reputational damage. Understanding why rejections happen — and how to prevent them — is the most valuable knowledge any Indian agro importer can have.

sections.caption: Laboratory testing at a NABL-accredited facility — HPLC-based pesticide residue analysis is the only method accepted by EU and US customs.

sections.heading: EU MRL vs US FDA: Understanding the Difference
sections.body: The EU MRL database (ec.europa.eu/food/plant/pesticides/eu-pesticides-database) sets specific limits for each pesticide-commodity combination. Default MRL in the EU is 0.01 mg/kg — lower than almost any other market. The USA (US FDA) uses tolerance levels set by EPA, which are generally more permissive than EU limits. A product that passes US FDA inspection may still fail at EU borders. The most commonly exceeded MRLs in Indian agro: chlorpyrifos (cumin, chilli), profenofos (chilli), cypermethrin (coriander), and aflatoxin B1 (groundnuts, chilli).

sections.label: NABL vs Non-NABL: A Critical Distinction
sections.text: EU and US customs authorities do not accept pesticide residue certificates from non-NABL-accredited laboratories. Rapid test kit results, in-house lab reports, and non-accredited third-party certificates are all insufficient. Always verify your supplier's NABL accreditation number at nabl.gov.in before accepting any COA. The NABL accreditation scope must specifically include the commodity class being tested (e.g., spices, oilseeds) and the specific analytes (LCMS, GCMS methods).

sections.heading: Radiance Overseas Three-Stage Testing Protocol
sections.body: To achieve zero EU RASFF alerts since our founding, Radiance Overseas implements testing at three stages: Stage 1 (Farm-gate): Rapid chlorpyrifos and aflatoxin screening before purchase decision. Stage 2 (Pre-processing): Full NABL LCMS pesticide panel (250+ analytes) on raw material before processing begins. Stage 3 (Pre-shipment): Full NABL panel on finished lot + aflatoxin HPLC + microbiological test. This protocol adds 5–7 days to production lead time but eliminates the financial and reputational risk of a RASFF alert. COAs from all three stages are provided to buyers on request.

title: Indian Black Pepper: Malabar vs Tellicherry vs Vietnamese — A Buyer's Technical Comparison
excerpt: Indian black pepper commands a premium over Vietnamese pepper due to higher piperine content and distinctive flavor profile. But what exactly differentiates Malabar Grade 1 (MG1) from Tellicherry Extra Bold (TGSEB)? We provide a technical comparison covering berry size, bulk density, piperine percentage, volatile oil content, and moisture levels across Indian, Vietnamese, Brazilian, and Indonesian origins. For food manufacturers blending pepper for retail or food service, we explain which origin and grade delivers the best cost-per-unit-of-piperine ratio. Current pricing and crop outlook for the 2025-26 Kerala harvest season are included.
author: Spice Export Desk

sections.heading: Indian Black Pepper: A Technical Buyer's Guide
sections.body: Black pepper is the world's most traded spice, and India — specifically the Malabar coast of Kerala — produces some of the world's highest-quality grades. Yet many international buyers purchasing 'Indian pepper' are unclear on the meaningful differences between Malabar Grade 1 (MG1), Tellicherry Extra Bold (TGSEB), and the Vietnam FAQ grade that dominates global volume trade. These distinctions matter enormously in food manufacturing, where piperine content and bulk density directly affect seasoning blend formulations and cost-per-unit-of-heat calculations.

sections.caption: Freshly harvested Tellicherry black pepper berries — the TGSEB grade is screen-sorted at >4.75mm and commands a global premium for its piperine density.

sections.heading: MG1 vs TGSEB vs Malabar FAQ: What the Grades Mean
sections.body: Malabar Grade 1 (MG1): Standard premium export grade. Screen size ≥4.0mm, bulk density ≥550 g/litre, moisture ≤12%, volatile oil ≥2ml/100g. Widely used in retail grinding and food service. Tellicherry Garbled Special Extra Bold (TGSEB): The premium grade. Screen size ≥4.75mm — only the largest berries qualify. Higher bulk density (≥600 g/litre), higher piperine content (5–7%), more intense aroma. For high-end retail brands and restaurant-quality grinding. Vietnam FAQ (Fairly Average Quality): Screen size ≥3.5mm, lower bulk density, piperine typically 2.5–4%. High volume, price-competitive, suitable for industrial blending where heat-unit economics matter more than aroma.

sections.label: Piperine Specification for Food Manufacturers
sections.text: When formulating pepper-containing products, specify piperine % as the primary quality metric, not just grade name. For hot sauces and strong seasonings where heat intensity matters: specify ≥5% piperine (TGSEB or premium MG1). For table-top retail grinding: ≥4% piperine, visual grade TGSEB for consumer appeal. For industrial food manufacturing blends: ≥3% piperine, MG1 or FAQ depending on price-performance target.

sections.heading: 2025-26 Kerala Crop and Price Outlook
sections.body: The Kerala black pepper harvest runs January through April. The 2025 crop was approximately 8% below average due to irregular rain in Wayanad and Idukki districts — the premium growing zones. This has kept TGSEB prices elevated versus Vietnam, with the traditional Indian premium widening from the usual 15–20% to approximately 25–30% currently. For food manufacturers who rely on Indian TGSEB for premium product lines, securing forward contracts at current levels before the June–September price peak is advisable. Radiance Overseas offers fixed-price 6-month supply contracts for volume buyers.

title: Millets Are the New Quinoa: India's International Year of Millets Legacy Drives Export Growth
excerpt: The UN's International Year of Millets (2023) catalyzed lasting demand shifts. India — the world's largest millet producer — has seen pearl millet (bajra), finger millet (ragi), and sorghum (jowar) exports grow 65% since 2022. Health-conscious consumers in the EU, USA, and Japan are driving demand for gluten-free millet flour, millet-based snacks, and millet breakfast cereals. We analyze which millet varieties have the strongest export potential, target markets and buyer profiles, organic certification pathways, and pricing competitiveness versus quinoa and oats. India's government incentives for millet processing and export are also covered.
author: Radiance Export Team

sections.heading: Millets: India's Ancient Grain Goes Global
sections.body: The UN International Year of Millets in 2023 acted as a global marketing platform for what Indian farmers have grown for millennia. The legacy is real and measurable: India's millet exports — pearl millet (bajra), finger millet (ragi), foxtail millet, and sorghum (jowar) — have grown 65% since 2022. EU health food retailers, Japanese functional food brands, and North American gluten-free cereal manufacturers are all driving this demand. Millets are nutritionally dense (higher protein, iron, and fibre than wheat), naturally gluten-free, drought-resistant, and organically certifiable at competitive prices.

sections.caption: Finger millet (ragi) harvest in Karnataka — increasingly exported to EU health food markets as a nutritious gluten-free grain.

sections.heading: Which Millet for Which Market
sections.body: Pearl Millet (Bajra): Highest production volume; used in animal feed and human food; growing demand as a flour ingredient in EU and US gluten-free bread. Protein 10–11%. Finger Millet (Ragi): Highest calcium content of any cereal; dark chocolate-brown colour; used in baby food, health cereals, malt beverages. Karnataka and Tamil Nadu origins. Foxtail Millet (Kangni): Growing export to Japan and South Korea; used in traditional rice-substitute dishes; mild flavour profile. Sorghum (Jowar): Largest global millet by volume; starch applications, gluten-free flour, brewery adjunct (craft sorghum beer in USA and UK is a growing market).

sections.label: Certification for the EU and US Health Food Market
sections.text: EU and US millet buyers overwhelmingly prefer organic certified product — and the price premium (15–30% over conventional) makes certification investment worthwhile. NPOP certification for millets from India is available via Control Union and Ecocert. For the US market, ensure your supplier has USDA NOP certification, not just NPOP. Radiance Overseas offers certified organic millet flour, whole grain, and flake formats.

sections.heading: Organic Millet Flour: The Fastest Growing Format
sections.body: While whole-grain millet has strong demand, millet flour is the fastest growing format — driven by gluten-free bakery, pasta, and snack applications. Sorghum flour, finger millet flour, and pearl millet flour are all being incorporated into artisan bread, flatbreads, and breakfast cereals in Europe and North America. India's milling capacity for millet flour has expanded significantly since 2023 with FSSAI-licensed mills now producing food-grade, NABL-tested flour. MOQ for millet flour from Radiance Overseas starts at 1 MT per variety.

title: Groundnut Export from India: Aflatoxin Compliance and How to Avoid EU RASFF Alerts
excerpt: Indian groundnuts are a $1 billion+ export category, but aflatoxin contamination remains the single biggest trade barrier. The EU's RASFF system flagged 23 Indian groundnut shipments in 2024 for aflatoxin B1 levels exceeding 2 ppb (total aflatoxin >4 ppb). We explain the science behind aflatoxin contamination in groundnuts, the critical role of pre-harvest and post-harvest management, how NABL-accredited lab testing with HPLC methodology differs from rapid test kits, and the specific sampling protocols (Commission Regulation 401/2006) that importers must understand. HPS (Hand Picked Selected) grade groundnuts with controlled cold chain storage consistently meet EU limits — we explain why.
author: Quality Assurance Team

sections.heading: India's Groundnut Export and the Aflatoxin Challenge
sections.body: Indian groundnuts are a USD 1 billion-plus export category with a persistent compliance problem: aflatoxin contamination is the single biggest trade barrier, particularly for EU-bound shipments. The EU's RASFF system flagged 23 Indian groundnut shipments in 2024 for aflatoxin B1 levels exceeding 2 ppb — the EU limit. Each rejection costs the exporter USD 3,000–12,000 in border inspection, re-export or destruction, and damaged buyer relationships. The science behind the problem is well understood; the solution lies in rigorous supply chain management from harvest to container.

sections.caption: HPS (Hand-Picked Selected) groundnuts — the premium grade that consistently meets EU aflatoxin limits through stringent sorting and cold-chain management.

sections.heading: Understanding Aflatoxin in Groundnuts
sections.body: Aflatoxin is produced by Aspergillus flavus and A. parasiticus moulds, primarily during post-harvest storage under warm, humid conditions. In Indian groundnuts, the critical contamination window is the 2–4 weeks between harvest and drying. If groundnuts are piled in the field or stored in jute bags without adequate ventilation and moisture control, aflatoxin levels can multiply from acceptable to non-compliant within days. EU Commission Regulation 401/2006 specifies the sampling protocol (a minimum of 10 incremental samples per 500 MT lot, composited to a 50kg aggregate sample) — most shipment rejections happen when this protocol is not followed.

sections.label: HPLC vs Rapid Test: Why It Matters
sections.text: EU customs laboratories use HPLC (High Performance Liquid Chromatography) for aflatoxin analysis — not lateral flow rapid test kits. Kits are useful for rapid field screening but cannot be used for compliance certification. Always request an HPLC-based aflatoxin analysis from a NABL-accredited lab before shipment. The NABL lab must have 'aflatoxin B1, B2, G1, G2' in its scope of accreditation — verify this at nabl.gov.in.

sections.heading: Why HPS Grade Consistently Meets EU Limits
sections.body: HPS (Hand-Picked Selected) groundnuts command a price premium because the selection process inherently removes aflatoxin risk. HPS selection involves manual inspection under UV light — aflatoxin-contaminated kernels fluoresce blue-green under 365nm UV, allowing contaminated nuts to be physically removed. Combined with cold-chain storage from harvest to container stuffing (temperature ≤20°C, RH ≤65%), HPS groundnuts from Rajasthan and Gujarat consistently achieve aflatoxin B1 well below 1 ppb — providing a safety buffer even accounting for sampling variability.

title: Instant Coffee Export from India: Freeze-Dried Arabica Is the Fastest Growing Segment
excerpt: India's instant coffee exports crossed $900 million in FY2025, with freeze-dried Arabica growing at 25% annually. Russia, CIS countries, Southeast Asia, and Africa are the largest markets, but EU and USA demand for premium freeze-dried is accelerating. We compare spray-dried (powder/agglomerated) vs freeze-dried (crystal) production processes, pricing per MT, shelf life, solubility, and aroma retention characteristics. For private-label buyers, we outline the MOQ, lead time, custom blend ratios (Arabica/Robusta), and packaging options available from Indian manufacturers. India now ranks as the world's 5th largest instant coffee exporter.
author: Coffee Sourcing Team

sections.heading: Freeze-Dried Coffee: India's Premium Instant Segment Takes Off
sections.body: India's instant coffee exports crossed USD 900 million in FY2025, and within that figure, the freeze-dried segment is growing at 25% annually — the fastest of any coffee format. The growth is being driven by consumer premiumisation across key markets: Russia, the CIS countries, and Southeast Asia are upgrading from spray-dried to freeze-dried; meanwhile, the EU and US premium grocery channels are adding Indian freeze-dried Arabica to private-label ranges. Freeze-dried coffee retains 85–92% of the volatile aroma compounds lost in spray-drying, making it the preferred format wherever cup quality matters.

sections.caption: Freeze-dried instant coffee crystals from a Karnataka facility — the irregular crystalline structure traps aroma compounds that spray-drying destroys.

sections.heading: Spray-Dried vs Freeze-Dried: Technical Comparison
sections.body: Spray-Dried Coffee: Hot-air drying at 150–200°C produces fine powder or agglomerated granules. Fast production, low cost, shelf life 18–24 months. Aroma retention: 30–40% of fresh extract. Suitable for: value-tier retail, vending machines, food service sachets. Freeze-Dried Coffee: Extract is frozen to -40°C then vacuum-dried at low temperature. Slow process (4–6 hours vs 30 seconds for spray). Produces irregular crystalline granules. Cost is 2.5–3.5x spray-dried. Aroma retention: 85–92%. Shelf life: 24–36 months in nitrogen-flushed packaging. Suitable for: premium retail, specialty grocery, private label brands.

sections.label: Private Label Specification Checklist
sections.text: For private-label freeze-dried coffee buyers: (1) Specify Arabica/Robusta blend ratio (common: 70/30, 80/20, 100% Arabica). (2) Moisture ≤4%, bulk density 150–250 g/litre for freeze-dried. (3) Particle size distribution for packaging consistency. (4) Packaging: nitrogen-flushed foil-lined jar or bag; oxygen absorber if requested. (5) MOQ: typically 3–5 MT for custom blends. Radiance Overseas can co-pack under your brand from 1,000 units with 500g fill.

sections.heading: Key Markets and Buyer Profiles for Indian Freeze-Dried
sections.body: Russia and CIS countries are the largest buyers of Indian instant coffee, accounting for ~35% of volume. These buyers are primarily upgrading from spray-dried to freeze-dried as consumer income levels rise. Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Vietnam domestic market, Philippines) is the second major growth market — particularly for freeze-dried Robusta blends. The EU and UK premium channel is the highest-growth opportunity for Indian freeze-dried Arabica — retailer private-label programmes are actively seeking Indian origin as a provenance story to differentiate from Brazilian and Vietnamese origins.

title: Mundra Port Overtakes JNPT: India's New #1 Container Port for Agro Exports
excerpt: Adani's Mundra port in Gujarat has officially overtaken JNPT (Nhava Sheva) as India's largest container port by TEU volume. For agro exporters, this shift has significant implications — Mundra offers faster turnaround times, lower CFS charges, and better connectivity to Gujarat and Rajasthan origins (sesame, cumin, groundnut, castor). We compare Mundra vs JNPT vs Chennai across five parameters that matter for agro commodity exporters: transit time to key destinations, shipping line coverage, refrigerated container availability, CFS and port charges, and container availability. Our recommendation: use Mundra for oil seeds, spices, and cereals; JNPT for rice; Chennai for coffee and South Indian products.
author: Logistics Team

sections.heading: Mundra Is Now India's Largest Container Port: What It Means for Agro Buyers
sections.body: Adani Ports' Mundra terminal in Gujarat has officially surpassed JNPT (Nhava Sheva) as India's largest container port by TEU throughput. For agro commodity exporters — particularly those shipping sesame, cumin, groundnut, and castor from Gujarat and Rajasthan origins — this shift has meaningful practical implications. Mundra now offers better shipping line frequency, lower dwell times, and significantly reduced container availability problems compared to JNPT, which has historically suffered from chassis shortages and CFS congestion.

sections.caption: Mundra Port, Gujarat — India's new largest container port with direct berthing for Post-Panamax vessels and superior CFS infrastructure.

sections.heading: Port Selection by Product Origin
sections.body: The optimal port is primarily determined by origin proximity, not destination preference. Use Mundra for: sesame (Unjha/Gujarat), cumin (Jodhpur/Rajasthan), castor seeds (Gujarat), groundnuts (Rajasthan/Gujarat), cotton, and most MP-origin products via the Dedicated Freight Corridor. Use JNPT for: Basmati rice from Punjab/Haryana, Maharashtra-origin dehydrated products, sugar, pulses from Latur belt. Use Chennai for: all South India products — coffee (Karnataka), black pepper (Kerala), tamarind, coconut, and South Indian spices. Mixing origin and port creates unnecessary inland freight costs.

sections.label: Container Availability: 2026 Update
sections.text: Container availability at Mundra has improved significantly vs 2021–22. Average empty container availability is 3–4 days from booking. For refrigerated containers (reefer), allow 7–10 days lead time. JNPT still has occasional equipment shortages during peak season (October–December). Book containers 10 days before intended stuffing date at either port to ensure availability.

sections.heading: CFS vs Factory Stuffing: Cost Comparison
sections.body: Factory stuffing (container taken to the processing facility and loaded directly) costs approximately 15–20% less than CFS (Container Freight Station) consolidation for full container loads. Factory stuffing also reduces double-handling damage risk and allows the Phytosanitary inspection officer to visit the factory directly rather than the CFS. At Mundra, factory stuffing from Gujarat origins is straightforward — the Dedicated Freight Corridor rail link from Ahmedabad to Mundra has dramatically reduced transit times and costs since 2022.

title: Moringa Powder Export: EU Novel Food Regulation and How Indian Suppliers Are Navigating It
excerpt: Moringa oleifera leaf powder is technically classified as a 'novel food' under EU Regulation 2015/2283, which means it requires pre-market authorization for sale in the EU. However, enforcement varies by member state, and several Indian exporters have successfully placed moringa products through the 'traditional food from third country' notification pathway. We explain the regulatory status in the EU, UK (post-Brexit rules differ), USA (GRAS status), and Australia/NZ, the required documentation for each market, and how organic certification (EU Organic + USDA NOP) strengthens the regulatory position. Current pricing, protein and iron content specifications, and major EU buyer profiles are included.
author: Herbs & Pharma Desk

sections.heading: Moringa and EU Novel Food Regulation: What Indian Exporters Need to Know
sections.body: Moringa oleifera leaf powder has become one of India's fastest-growing herbal exports — and simultaneously one of its most complicated regulatory situations. In the EU, moringa leaf powder is classified as a 'novel food' under Regulation (EU) 2015/2283, meaning it requires pre-market authorisation for sale as a food supplement. However, enforcement varies considerably by EU member state, and several Indian exporters have successfully placed products through the 'traditional food from a third country' notification pathway. Understanding exactly where moringa sits in each market's regulatory framework before shipping is essential.

sections.caption: Fresh moringa leaves harvested in Tamil Nadu — India supplies over 60% of global moringa leaf powder exports.

sections.heading: EU, UK, US, Australia: Regulatory Status by Market
sections.body: In the EU: Moringa leaf powder as a food supplement requires novel food authorisation unless the 'traditional food from third country' pathway applies. Several products have been notified successfully — your supplier should be able to confirm if their product has a notification in place. In the UK (post-Brexit): Regulated under the UK Novel Food and Special Foods legislation; separate from EU — a UK-specific traditional food notification is required. In the USA: Moringa is not on the FDA GRAS list, but it can be sold as a dietary supplement under DSHEA without pre-approval (seller assumes responsibility for safety). In Australia/NZ: FSANZ Novel Food application is required for moringa as a novel ingredient.

sections.label: Documentation for EU Moringa Buyers
sections.text: For EU-bound moringa powder, require from your supplier: (1) Confirmation that the specific product has a valid EU Novel Food notification or is sold under an existing authorisation. (2) NABL COA covering protein %, moisture, heavy metals (ICP-MS), microbiological, and pesticide residues. (3) NPOP organic certificate if sold as organic. (4) Phytosanitary certificate. Never import moringa into the EU without confirming the novel food status in the destination member state first.

sections.heading: Quality Specifications for Moringa Leaf Powder
sections.body: Premium moringa leaf powder specifications: Protein ≥25% (key selling point for nutritional buyers), moisture ≤8%, colour dark green (avoid yellowing from excessive drying temperature — keep drying temperature ≤45°C), particle size 60–80 mesh, TPC ≤10⁵ CFU/g, Salmonella absent, heavy metals per USP 2232 limits. Tamil Nadu and Rajasthan origins both produce good quality; Tamil Nadu moringa typically has higher protein content due to variety and climate.

title: Toor Dal Prices Soar 45%: India's Production Shortfall Creates Export Opportunity for Lentils
excerpt: India's domestic toor dal (pigeon pea) prices have risen 45% in 2025 due to consecutive poor monsoon seasons in Maharashtra and Karnataka. While this has triggered government intervention in the domestic market, it has simultaneously opened an export window for masoor dal (red lentils) and moong dal, which are in surplus. For international buyers, this creates an arbitrage opportunity — Indian masoor dal is currently 15-20% cheaper than Canadian red lentils on a CIF basis for Middle Eastern and South Asian destinations. We analyze the export policy implications, available surplus estimates, quality specifications, and why lentil importers should act before the next kharif season changes the equation.
author: Pulses Export Team

sections.heading: Toor Dal Price Surge: The Opportunity Hidden in the Crisis
sections.body: India's domestic toor dal (pigeon pea) prices rose 45% in 2025, driven by back-to-back poor monsoon seasons in Maharashtra and Karnataka — the two dominant toor-growing states. The government responded with import facilitation and domestic market intervention, suppressing further price escalation but not eliminating the underlying supply deficit. For international buyers, the toor dal crisis has created an indirect opportunity: Indian exporters sitting on surplus masoor dal (red lentils) and moong dal are offering these at competitive prices as they redirect capacity. Indian masoor is currently 15–20% cheaper CIF than Canadian red lentils to Middle Eastern ports.

sections.caption: Masoor dal (red lentils) — currently in surplus and available at competitive prices versus Canadian origin for Middle East and South Asia markets.

sections.heading: The Masoor Arbitrage: Why Act Now
sections.body: Canadian red lentils have been the default procurement choice for many Middle Eastern and South Asian importers for over a decade — consistency, reliable quality, and strong logistics infrastructure made Canada the safe choice. In 2026, Indian masoor dal offers a compelling alternative: the CIF price differential of 15–20% is the widest in years, protein content is comparable (24–26% dry basis), and quality from the Bihar-UP production belt has improved significantly with better drying and cleaning infrastructure. The window is time-limited: the kharif 2026 crop will determine whether India remains in surplus from August onwards.

sections.label: Quality Specification for Red Lentils
sections.text: For masoor dal export, specify: moisture ≤12%, broken ≤1%, foreign matter ≤0.1%, bright uniform orange-red colour (reject any yellow or brown discolouration — it indicates old stock), pesticide residue test (particularly for dimethoate and chlorpyrifos). Request packing in new food-grade BOPP bags — not re-used jute — to preserve colour and avoid contamination claims at destination.

sections.heading: Moong Dal Procurement Opportunity
sections.body: Green moong (whole) and moong dal split are both in good supply from Rajasthan and MP in 2026. Sprouting-grade green moong exports to Southeast Asia (primarily Thailand, Malaysia, and Vietnam for bean sprout production) have been running at record volumes. For EU and North American health food buyers, certified organic moong dal is available with NPOP and USDA NOP certification. Protein content 23–25%, naturally rich in folate and potassium — strong nutritional positioning for the health food segment.

title: Private Label Packaging for Indian Agro Products: What Importers Need to Specify
excerpt: An increasing number of European and American importers are sourcing Indian rice, spices, and pulses for their own private label brands. But the specification process is complex — packaging material (PP, BOPP, jute, vacuum, nitrogen-flushed), labeling regulations (EU FIC 1169/2011, FDA 21 CFR 101), barcode formats (EAN-13 vs UPC-A), nutritional panel requirements, allergen declarations, and country-of-origin labeling all vary by destination market. We provide a complete specification checklist that importers should share with their Indian supplier, common mistakes that cause customs delays, and the typical MOQ and lead time for private label orders. Radiance Overseas offers private label packaging from 500g retail packs to 50kg bulk bags with custom branding.
author: Export Documentation Team

sections.heading: Private Label Indian Agro Products: Getting the Specification Right
sections.body: Private label sourcing from India has become mainstream among European and American food retailers. Indian Basmati rice, organic spices, and premium lentils now appear under retailer own-brand labels at Tesco, Whole Foods, Carrefour, and dozens of specialty chains. But the specification process is more complex than most first-time buyers anticipate. From packaging material selection to country-of-origin labelling regulations, from allergen declaration formats to barcode standards — getting any one element wrong can delay customs clearance or trigger a product recall.

sections.caption: Private label specification document — European and US retailers have detailed packaging and labelling requirements that Indian exporters must meet precisely.

sections.heading: Packaging Materials: Matching Format to Product
sections.body: Packaging material selection is product-specific: Basmati rice: Transparent BOPP (biaxially oriented polypropylene) for retail 1kg/2kg/5kg — consumers want to see grain quality. Jute bags for 25–50kg institutional. Spices (powder): Multi-layer foil-laminate pouches with zip-lock; barrier against moisture and oxidation is critical for powders. Sesame, pulses: PP woven bags for 25–50kg B2B, vacuum-sealed BOPP for retail. Dehydrated vegetables: Nitrogen-flushed foil bags — oxygen is the enemy of dehy products. All retail packaging must meet the destination country's food contact material regulations (EU Regulation 10/2011 for plastics).

sections.label: EU vs US Labelling: The Key Differences
sections.text: EU (FIC 1169/2011): Mandatory 14 allergen declarations, nutrition declaration per 100g, 'Best Before' date, net quantity, country of origin, business operator name/address. US (FDA 21 CFR 101): Nutrition Facts Panel (per serving + per container), allergens per FALCPA, 'Best By' date, net quantity in US customary + metric, country of origin for certain commodities. EAN-13 barcode for EU; UPC-A for USA. If you ship to both, request dual labelling — Radiance Overseas can print combined labels.

sections.heading: MOQ, Lead Time, and Print Setup
sections.body: For private label orders from Radiance Overseas: Retail packs (500g–2kg): MOQ 500 units per SKU; 4–6 weeks lead time including print approval cycle. Institutional (25–50kg): MOQ 1 MT per SKU; 2–3 weeks. Print setup fee: One-time USD 150–250 per SKU for flexo plate — waived on reorders. Approved artwork: Provide print-ready PDF with all text in final form; Radiance's design team can create artwork from a brand brief at cost. First production run requires pre-shipment sample approval before bulk production.

title: Sella vs Steam vs Raw Basmati: A Visual and Technical Guide for First-Time Rice Importers
excerpt: If you are importing Indian Basmati rice for the first time, the terminology can be confusing. What exactly is the difference between Raw (white milled), Sella (parboiled), and Steam (lightly steamed) Basmati? This comprehensive guide explains the processing differences with visual comparisons, how each type behaves during cooking (elongation ratio, stickiness, texture), which markets prefer which type (Middle East prefers Sella, EU/USA prefer Raw, South Asia prefers Steam), pricing differences per MT, and shelf life considerations. We cover this for all four major Basmati varieties: 1121, 1718, Pusa, and 1509. Container loading capacity and standard quality specifications (broken %, moisture, whiteness) for each processing type are included in a downloadable comparison table.
author: Rice Export Desk

sections.heading: Sella vs Steam vs Raw Basmati: The Complete Beginner's Guide
sections.body: First-time Basmati importers consistently find the processing type terminology confusing — and understandably so. 'Sella', 'Steam', and 'Raw' are not brand names or variety names; they describe how the paddy was processed after harvest. Each processing type produces a dramatically different cooked rice experience, suits different end markets, and carries different shelf-life implications. Getting this choice right is the most important single decision a first-time rice buyer makes — the wrong processing type for your market will generate customer complaints and returns regardless of grain quality.

sections.caption: Three processing types side by side: Raw (white), Sella Cream (light gold), and Sella Golden (amber) — visually and texturally distinct.

sections.heading: Raw (White Milled) Basmati
sections.body: Raw Basmati is the most natural form — paddy is milled directly without any soaking or steaming. The resulting grain is brilliant white, fragile, and cooks to the longest possible elongation. The aroma is at its purest and most intense. However, raw Basmati has the shortest shelf life (12–15 months under ambient conditions), is most susceptible to breakage in transit, and requires careful moisture control in storage. EU, US, and UK consumers and premium Middle Eastern buyers prefer raw for the authentic Basmati eating experience. The cooked grain should double or triple in length and separate completely.

sections.label: Which Market Wants Which Processing Type?
sections.text: Middle East (GCC): Sella Golden or Sella Cream — long shelf life suits trading hub distribution; UAE and Saudi prefer Sella. EU and North America: Raw White — premium grocery and health food buyers want authentic Basmati characteristics. South Asia (Bangladesh, Sri Lanka): Steam Basmati — faster cook time suits restaurant and home cooking patterns. Africa: Mostly non-basmati parboiled but where Basmati is sold, Sella is preferred.

sections.heading: Sella Cream vs Sella Golden
sections.body: Sella Cream is parboiled at lower pressure/temperature, resulting in a light cream-to-white colour that closely resembles raw rice visually. It has better head rice recovery than raw (3–5% higher), improved shelf life (18–24 months), and slightly reduced aroma intensity. Popular in GCC countries where visual similarity to raw is valued. Sella Golden is parboiled at higher pressure, resulting in the characteristic amber-gold colour. Superior shelf life (24–36 months), very high head rice recovery (≥70%), much firmer cooked texture — grains separate easily even after reheating. Preferred for biryani and pilaf preparations.

Buyers who plan quarterly procurement windows aligned to harvest cycles consistently achieve 8–12% lower annual average costs than those who buy reactively.

sections.text: India's pulse export policy changes frequently. Always confirm the current export status (free/restricted/floor price) with your exporter before signing. Radiance Overseas provides real-time export policy updates to registered buyers.

sections.heading: India's Export Infrastructure: What Buyers Should Know
sections.body: India's pulse supply chain has a distinct seasonal character that experienced importers exploit for cost savings. The kharif (summer) crop harvests from November through January, and the rabi (winter) crop from March through May. For moong and urad, the kharif window offers the best prices. For chana, gram, and masoor, the rabi window is optimal. Year-round supply is possible through trader inventory, but at higher prices. Buyers who plan quarterly procurement windows aligned to harvest cycles consistently achieve 8–12% lower annual average costs than those who buy reactively.

sections.label: Transaction Certificate Reminder
sections.text: The annual organic certificate proves the supplier is certified — it does NOT cover your specific shipment. The Transaction Certificate (TC) must be issued for each shipment by the NPOP-accredited certifying body and must match your Bill of Lading exactly.

sections.heading: India's Export Infrastructure: What Buyers Should Know
sections.body: India's organic certification ecosystem has grown to over 30 NPOP-accredited certifying bodies covering all major agricultural states. The most widely recognised bodies for international trade are Control Union, ECOCERT, Lacon, and OneCert — their NPOP certificates are accepted at EU, UK, and Canadian borders without additional verification. For USDA NOP, US-accredited certifiers operating in India include CCOF, QCS, and OEFFA. When verifying a supplier's organic certificate, always check: (1) the certificate is current, (2) the specific product category is in scope, (3) the certifying body is on the NPOP accredited list, and (4) the TC for your shipment has been issued.

sections.label: Sourcing Tip
sections.text: Request moisture content (target 10–12%), screen size distribution, and defect count (SCAA methodology) for all Indian green coffee. For EU organic buyers, the NPOP Transaction Certificate must be issued per shipment — not just per year.

sections.heading: India's Export Infrastructure: What Buyers Should Know
sections.body: India's coffee sector is undergoing a generational shift. Estate owners — many third or fourth generation — are investing in processing infrastructure, international cupping certifications (Q Grader), and direct trade relationships with roasters globally. The Coffee Board of India's Specialty Coffee programme provides market access support including participation in international trade shows and lot promotion through online platforms. For importers considering an India direct-sourcing programme, this is the optimal moment: supply is growing, quality is improving, and pricing remains competitive versus Central American specialty origins.

sections.label: Heavy Metals
sections.text: For all Indian herbs going to EU and US supplement markets, require an ICP-MS heavy metals test (As, Cd, Pb, Hg) from a NABL-accredited lab. Heavy metal limits in herbs are strict — and some Indian growing regions have naturally elevated soil levels.

sections.heading: India's Export Infrastructure: What Buyers Should Know
sections.body: India's herbal export sector has developed a two-tier quality structure. The organised tier — FSSAI-licensed processors, NABL lab testing, organic certification — produces reliably compliant products at premium prices. The unorganised tier produces cheaper product that regularly fails EU and US testing. The price difference between tiers is typically 20–40%, but the risk difference is orders of magnitude greater. For buyers supplying supplement brands, health food retailers, or pharmaceutical companies, sourcing exclusively from the organised tier is non-negotiable — a single failed batch can trigger a brand recall.
